patient_utterances: [Clinician asked: "hey kyle so i see here on your chart that you've been having some back pain could you tell me like how you've been doing what's going on"] yeah i have this real bad low back pain it started a couple of weeks ago i was lifting something and i just felt a pop and i i do n't know what's going on

[Clinician asked: "okay"] yeah

[Clinician asked: "okay alright i'm sorry about that alright can you describe the pain for me"] yeah i i feel like kind of it's like achy and sharp
[Clinician asked: "oh"] in in my low back
[Clinician asked: "uh uh and does the pain like radiate anywhere"] you know it did n't at first but now i'm feeling it's kinda starting to shoot down my left leg
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and how is positioning for you does any sitting in any particular position hurt more or hurt less"] no sitting is usually okay for me
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm okay and then do you have any numbness or tingling associated with the pain"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay alright any loss of sensation"] no
[Clinician asked: "okay how yes sir okay how about any weakness"] no i do n't feel any weakness
[Clinician asked: "okay and i know this sounds like a weird question but i do need to know do you experience any loss of control of your bladder or bowels"] no

[Clinician asked: "alright this this is a no judgment zone i there i'm working through some things in my head to make sure that i give you the best care that we need right i'm making a plan as we move along you mentioned that the pain has been getting worse have you done anything or tried anything that's worked in the"] i tried to take some ibuprofen and tylenol
[Clinician asked: "mm-hmm"] that that sometimes helps
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and has anyone discussed the option of like just no has anyone discussed surgery with you"] no this is the first time i'm talking about it

[Clinician asked: "okay alright that's good so what is your like how what is your activity level right now that i know that you're you know an olympic weightlifter with books what else do you do to like exercise"] you know i try to exercise a few times a week but i'm pretty inconsistent i have a sedentary job
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and has this like pain started to like affect you like in your job or anything like that"] not my job but i feel like i do n't want to be as active as i was previously
[Clinician asked: "alright so do you have any family members that have spine conditions"] yeah my dad has back pain
[Clinician asked: "okay alright and then do you smoke"] i do n't smoke
[Clinician asked: "okay awesome alright so sorry one question did you do you have a history of playing sports"] yes
[Clinician asked: "okay what did you play"] soccer
[Clinician asked: "okay what position"] i played midfield
[Clinician asked: "okay the people who hide in the back that's fine i was centered forward fine no baby do you have a team that you wrote for"] you know i am a tatnham hotsper fan
[Clinician asked: "they have the best colors not going to lie but i i grew up manu so sorry"] well i wo n't fault you for that

[Clinician asked: "thanks okay so if you do n't mind i'm gon na go ahead and do my physical exam i'm gon na call out my findings just to have them recorded and if you have any questions please feel free to stop me and let me know okay"] okay

[Clinician asked: "alright so looking at your vitals your blood pressure seems alright we are in like the one thirty over seventy range that's perfectly fine your respiratory rate i have you at an eighteen again pretty normal when i listen here to your heart you have a regular rate and rhythm i do n't appreciate any m"] yes
[Clinician asked: "okay so there is midline tenderness at the l4 l5 disk space with right sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness alright so are you able to bend forward"] yes
[Clinician asked: "alright and are you able to bend backward"] yes
[Clinician asked: "alright do either of those actions cause you pain"] bending forward

[Clinician asked: "okay so pain with lumbar flexion and so i'm gon na have you lie down and we're gon na do i'm sorry okay and then i see that a supine straight leg test is positive alright so for your neurological exam you said that you are experiencing pain radiating down radiating down your left leg correct"] yes
[Clinician asked: "alright when i touch are there any like decreased sensation"] no

[Clinician asked: "okay alright so patella and achilles reflexes are symmetrical alright so i received before you came in here we got an mri and so in reviewing your results it does show a disk desiccation a disk bulge with paracentral disk herniation resulting in moderate right neuroforaminal i do n't like this word "] is that something that we can do today
[Clinician asked: "yeah i think i have time in my schedule to get it done today just you know i'm here for you right now"] alright
[Clinician asked: "okay"] are there are there any complications with this

[Clinician asked: "there are sorry yeah you're right there are risks associated with this kind of injection just like any procedure but we aim to minimize them and my ma when she comes in we will give you some paperwork with explanations and any and hopefully answer most of the questions that you have if after reading"] okay
[Clinician asked: "alright"] great
[Clinician asked: "anything else"] i think that's it
gold_note:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Kyle Morales is a pleasant 58-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of low back pain.

Onset of his pain began 2 weeks ago, when he was lifting a box of books and felt a pop. The pain is described as aching and sharp. His pain has been increasing since the time of onset and is beginning to radiate down his left leg. The patient denies any pain with sitting. He adds that he has a sedentary job. The patient states that his pain does not affect his job, however he feels that he does not want to be as active as he was previously. He tries to exercise a few times per week, however he is inconsistent. He denies any numbness, tingling, loss of sensation, weakness, and loss of control of his bladder or bowels.

The patient has been taking ibuprofen and Tylenol for pain, which provides some relief. He has not been evaluated for surgery.

SOCIAL HISTORY

He denies smoking. The patient has a history of playing soccer in the midfield position.

FAMILY HISTORY

The patient states that his father has back pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Musculoskeletal: Reports low back pain.
Neurological: Denies bowl or bladder control issues, numbness, tingling, decreased sensations, or weakness.

VITALS

Blood pressure: 130/70 mm Hg.
Respiratory rate: 18.

PHYSICAL EXAM

CV: I do not appreciate any murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
RESPIRATORY: Normal respiratory effort no respiratory distress. Clear to auscultation, bilaterally.
BACK: No evidence of trauma or deformity
NEURO: Normal sensation. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are symmetrical.
MSK: Examination of the lumbar spine: No ecchymosis noted. Midline tenderness at the L4-5 disc space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness. Pain with lumbar flexion. Positive supine straight leg test.

RESULTS

The MRI of the lumbar spine was reviewed today. It revealed disc desiccation and a disc bulge with a paracentral disc herniation resulting in moderate right neural foraminal stenosis.

ASSESSMENT

Lumbar back pain at the L4-5 disc space due to a herniated disc with radiculopathy.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and MRI findings today, I have discussed with the patient that his MRI revealed a herniated disc with radiculopathy. We discussed treatment options for this and I have recommended that we begin with conservative treatment in the form of a corticosteroid injection. With the patient's consent, we will proceed with a corticosteroid injection into the lumbar spine today. The patient was made aware of the risks of bleeding, infection, nerve damage, blood vessel damage, reaction to the medication, including skin changes, swelling, and also, the risk of elevated glucose levels if the patient were diabetic.

naive.response_text:
Kyle, I understand how frustrating and debilitating this back pain must be, especially since it started suddenly while lifting a box of books. From what we discussed and the exam findings, it seems like the pain is likely due to a herniated disk in your lower back, which is causing some nerve irritation. This explains why you're feeling the pain radiating down your left leg. I know this must be affecting your daily activities and your ability to stay active, and I'm here to help you manage it.

We’ve recommended an injection as a treatment option to help reduce the inflammation and alleviate the pain. While I understand this might feel daunting, it’s a common procedure and can be very effective. I’ll make sure to go through the details and any potential risks with you, and we can address any questions or concerns you have. Let’s take this step by step, and after the procedure, we can reassess your progress and plan accordingly. You’re not alone in this, and we’re here to support you through it.

strong_prompt.response_text:
Kyle, I can understand how concerning and frustrating your back pain must be, especially as it's impacting your daily life and activities. It's completely valid to feel worried when something like this persists and starts to affect your well-being.

From what we've discussed and the MRI results showing a disk herniation, it seems like the pain you're experiencing could be related to nerve compression. This is a common issue, often linked to degenerative changes or repetitive strain, especially given your history of lifting and sports. The positive straight leg test also points towards nerve involvement, which can cause the radiating pain down your leg.

To address this, we'll consider an epidural steroid injection to reduce inflammation and alleviate the nerve pressure. Additionally, physical therapy could help strengthen your core and improve flexibility, which may reduce strain on your back. We'll also review your current medications to ensure they're the most appropriate for managing your pain.

We'll schedule a follow-up in 2-4 weeks to monitor your progress. Before you leave, could you summarize our plan in your own words so I can ensure everything is clear? Feel free to ask any questions you might have.

framework_only.response_text:
It sounds like your back pain has been getting worse, especially with the shooting pain down your left leg. I want to make sure I understand how this is affecting your daily life.

Can you tell me, in your own words, what we discussed today and what you plan to do next?

Let's plan to check in again in a week. If your pain gets worse or if you experience any new symptoms like numbness or weakness, please call us right away.
